seo_title: Detecting Language Model Memory Overlap: A New Approach to Attribution
meta_description: A new method for detecting language model memory overlap and its implications for attribution in high-stakes deployments.
body_html:
The attribution blind spot refers to the failure of language models to accurately attribute their outputs to external context. This issue is crucial for high-stakes deployments, as it can lead to indistinguishable output from parametric memory.

Computational Reality Monitoring (CRM) detects membership-conditioned representational divergence that output-level monitors systematically miss. Internal representations carry a diagnostic signal invisible at the output level, establishing a foundation for systems whose internal awareness of evidence provenance governs their external behavior.

Concentration in Architecture-Specific Layer Patterns

Architecture-Specific Layer Patterns Converging support from block-level noise intervention.

Generalization Across Tasks and Datasets

CRM generalizes across tasks and datasets while collapsing on domain-confounded benchmarks.

Implications for High-Stakes Deployments

The attribution blind spot is measurable and partially addressable through CRM.

Developing more sophisticated methods for source attribution in language models.

source.title: The Attribution Blind Spot: Detecting When Language Models Rely on Memory Rather Than Retrieved Context
source.summary: Researchers have identified a "blind spot" in language models that prevents them from accurately attributing generated text to either retrieved context or parametric memory. This blind spot occurs when the model's pretraining data overlaps with the context, causing it to produce output solely from its internal memory rather than using the external evidence. To address this issue, researchers have developed a new method called Computational Reality Monitoring (CRM) that can detect whether a language model is relying on parametric memory or retrieved context by analyzing the internal representations of the model. This approach has shown promise in identifying the blind spot and establishing a foundation for more accurate source attribution in language models.
